tier_1_regimens.notes: The longest duration of prophylaxis against SCC has been demonstrated with topical Fluorouracil plus Calcipotriol [3], [4].
Fluorouracil is a preferred topical option for field cancerization [1], [2].
Imiquimod is an accepted topical option for field cancerization [7], [8].
Tirbanibulin is an accepted topical option for actinic keratoses [165].
Diclofenac is an option for field cancerization with category 2B evidence [207-209].
Capecitabine may be considered for severe refractory disease that has progressed on oral retinoids [6].
Photodynamic therapy with aminolevulinic acid is an option for field cancerization [166-174].
Photodynamic therapy with porfimer sodium may be considered for field cancerization [243].
Oral retinoids like acitretin are therapeutic options to reduce the development of actinic keratoses, but side effects may be significant [5].
Oral isotretinoin is an option for chemoprophylaxis in high-risk patients [498].

tier_1_regimens.site: Locally Advanced CSCC, Regional Lymph Node Disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin is a preferred systemic therapy option for use with RT in locally advanced or regional CSCC [9].

tier_1_regimens.site: Locally Advanced CSCC, Regional Lymph Node Disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin is an other recommended option for use with RT in locally advanced or regional CSCC.

tier_1_regimens.site: Locally Advanced CSCC, Regional Lymph Node Disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel is an other recommended combination for use with RT in locally advanced or regional CSCC [13], [14].

tier_1_regimens.site: Locally Advanced CSCC, Regional Lymph Node Disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: EGFR inhibitors like cetuximab are other recommended options for use with RT in locally advanced or regional CSCC [15].

tier_1_regimens.site: Locally Advanced CSCC, Regional Lymph Node Disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/fluorouracil may be used in certain circumstances with RT for locally advanced or regional CSCC [22].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for systemic therapy when curative RT or surgery not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cemiplimab-rwlc is a preferred option for systemic therapy alone in locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC when curative RT or surgery is not feasible [10], [11].

tier_1_regimens.setting: First-line for systemic therapy when curative RT or surgery not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is a preferred option for systemic therapy alone in locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC when curative RT or surgery is not feasible [12].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Systemic therapy when curative RT or surgery not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cosibelimab-ipd is an other recommended option for locally advanced (category 2B) or metastatic CSCC when curative RT or surgery is not feasible [16].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Systemic therapy when curative RT or surgery not feasible
tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab is an other recommended option for systemic therapy alone in locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC [459].

tier_1_regimens.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel is an option for patients ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors [17].

tier_1_regimens.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel plus cetuximab is an option for patients ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors [18-21].

tier_1_regimens.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors
tier_1_regimens.notes: EGFR inhibitors like cetuximab are options for patients ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors [15].
Neoadjuvant cemiplimab-rwlc may be considered for borderline resectable, unresectable, or high morbidity surgery cases [10].

tier_1_regimens.site: Very-High-Risk CSCC with nodal and non-nodal features

drugs.name: Cemiplimab-rwlc
drugs.dose: 350 mg every 3 weeks for 12 weeks, then 700 mg every 6 weeks for up to 36 weeks

trial_data.trial_name: Adjuvant Cemiplimab or Placebo in High-Risk CSCC

tier_1_regimens.notes: Adjuvant cemiplimab-rwlc is category 1 preferred for patients with extremely high-risk nodal and non-nodal features following adjuvant RT [c].

tier_1_regimens.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Capecitabine may be considered for patients ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors [23], [24].

tier_1_regimens.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin may be considered for patients ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors.

tier_1_regimens.setting: After progression on immune checkpoint inhibitors
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/fluorouracil may be considered for patients ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors [24].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Regional therapy for unresectable limb disease
tier_1_regimens.notes: ILI/ILP with melphalan-based regimens is useful in certain circumstances for limb-only S-ITM [bbb].
Oral nicotinamide may reduce the development of CSCCs [t].

tier_2_handbook.definition: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC or SCC) is a malignant neoplasm arising from epidermal keratinocytes, representing the second most common skin cancer after basal cell carcinoma [1,2]. It originates from the squamous cells of the epidermis and can develop in situ (Bowen's disease) or as invasive carcinoma [3]. CSCC most frequently occurs on sun-exposed skin surfaces, with the head and neck being the most common site (accounting for the majority of cases), followed by the hands, forearms, and lower legs [4,5]. The anatomical boundaries extend from the epidermis through the dermis and can invade deeper structures including subcutaneous fat, muscle, cartilage, or bone in advanced cases [3,6]. For staging purposes, the AJCC 8th edition staging system is used for cutaneous carcinoma of the head and neck, defining Tis as carcinoma in situ, with invasive tumors staged from T1 to T4 based on size (>2 cm, >4 cm), depth of invasion (>6 mm or beyond subcutaneous fat), and involvement of structures like bone, nerves, or skull base [7,8]. CSCC can also arise in areas of chronic inflammation, scars (Marjolin's ulcer), or prior radiation therapy [4,9]. The disease can be locally destructive, cause disfigurement, and in rare cases (estimated 1.9% to 2.7% of all CSCC) metastasize to regional lymph nodes or distant sites [10,11].

subtypes.name: Conventional Squamous Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: The most common histologic type, characterized by keratinocyte atypia with varying degrees of differentiation (well, moderate, or poorly differentiated).
subtypes.frequency: Most common subtype (exact frequency not specified in source)

subtypes.name: Adenosquamous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A rare and aggressive variant with both squamous and glandular (adenomatous) differentiation.
subtypes.key_associations: Considered high-risk histology associated with increased risk of recurrence and metastasis

subtypes.name: Sarcomatoid (Spindle Cell) Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A high-risk variant composed of spindle-shaped cells with epithelial-mesenchymal transition, often with desmoplastic stroma.
subtypes.key_associations: High-risk histology, aggressive behavior, perineural invasion

subtypes.name: Verrucous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A low-risk, well-differentiated variant with a warty, exophytic growth pattern and minimal invasive potential.
subtypes.key_associations: Low metastatic potential, may be associated with HPV

subtypes.name: Acantholytic (Adenoid) Squamous Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by loss of intercellular bridges leading to a pseudoglandular appearance.
subtypes.key_associations: Reportable histologic feature, may be associated with hair follicles

subtypes.name: Basosquamous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A tumor with features of both basal cell carcinoma and squamous cell carcinoma, often behaving more aggressively like CSCC.
subtypes.key_associations: May behave aggressively, requires careful staging

subtypes.name: Infiltrative Squamous Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Characterized by irregular, angulated, and infiltrative growth patterns into the dermis and deeper tissues.
subtypes.frequency: Reported as a histologic feature (exact frequency not specified)
subtypes.key_associations: Higher risk of recurrence and metastasis

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The provided NCCN guideline does not detail specific genomic events, driver mutations, or signaling pathways in the molecular pathogenesis of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma. It primarily focuses on clinical risk factors and management. However, the pathogenesis is understood to be driven by cumulative ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure, which induces DNA damage, particularly in key tumor suppressor genes such as TP53 and oncogenes like RAS. In immunocompromised individuals (e.g., organ transplant recipients), additional pathways involving immune surveillance evasion and possibly viral oncogenesis (e.g., HPV) may contribute to the more aggressive behavior observed [4,5]. The document notes that CSCC develops more frequently and behaves more aggressively in settings of immunosuppression, which is associated with distinct clinicopathologic features such as diffuse/focal spindle cell morphology, HPV infection, and aggressive subclinical extension [19,20]. Genetic syndromes like xeroderma pigmentosum (XP) and recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa (RDEB) dramatically predispose patients to aggressive CSCC due to defective DNA repair or structural integrity mechanisms [21,22].

epidemiology.incidence: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma is the second most common skin cancer. Current estimates indicate that approximately 3.3 million individuals in the United States have ≥1 basal or squamous cell skin cancers, with SCCs accounting for about 20% of these [1,23]. Numerous population-based studies demonstrate that the incidence of SCC is rising, and some studies show it is rising more rapidly than basal cell carcinoma [24-26].
epidemiology.mortality: CSCC generally has a good prognosis, with a 5-year overall survival rate of about 98% [1,27,28]. However, it can produce substantial local destruction and disfigurement. Metastatic disease is rare, estimated at 1.9% to 2.7% of all CSCC, with nodal metastases and distant metastatic disease estimated at 3.7% and 0.4%, respectively [10,11].
epidemiology.trends: The incidence of SCC is increasing in many populations worldwide, including the United States, Europe, and Australia [24-26,29]. This increase may be due to improved detection, an aging population, and ongoing UV exposure.
epidemiology.demographics: The incidence of UV-induced SCC is very low in non-white populations and increases with age, particularly in sun-exposed sites [4,30,31]. SCC is more common in individuals with light skin, hair, and eye color who have had extensive sun exposure [32,33]. There are significant occupational disparities, with higher rates in outdoor workers [34,35]. Indoor tanning is significantly associated with SCC, increasing the risk by 67% [36,37].

risk_factors.detail: The most recognized environmental carcinogen. Chronic sun exposure, total site-specific exposure, and number of site-specific sunburns are strongly correlated with development of SCC.
Any exposure to indoor tanning increases the risk of SCC by 67%.
Individuals with light skin, hair, and eye color are at greatest risk for UV-induced SCC.
AKs and Bowen's disease (SCC in situ), if left untreated, can progress to invasive SCC.

Immunosuppression (e.g., organ transplantation, lymphoma, CLL, drug-induced, HIV) is a major risk factor. Transplant recipients have a 5-fold to 113-fold increased incidence of SCC compared to the general population.
SCC can develop in scars from burns, chronic ulcers, or other injuries, known as Marjolin's ulcer. These lesions tend to be difficult to treat and have higher risk of recurrence.
Rare genetic syndromes greatly predispose individuals to SCC, including xeroderma pigmentosum (XP), recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa (RDEB), and albinism.
SCC arising in areas previously irradiated for unrelated conditions is a risk factor for recurrence or metastasis.
SCC arising from sites of chronic scarring or inflammation has increased rates of metastasis.
While not explicitly quantified in this guideline, SCC incidence is generally higher in males, likely due to greater cumulative sun exposure.
Incidence increases with age, reflecting cumulative UV exposure.

protective_factors: Regular sunscreen use (reduces rate of development of new AKs and CSCCs) [57,58]
Oral nicotinamide (may reduce development of CSCCs by enhancing DNA repair and reducing UV-induced immunosuppression) [59,60]
Aggressive treatment of precancers (can prevent development of subsequent invasive tumors) [41]
tier_2_handbook.clinical_features: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC) typically presents as a sun-exposed skin lesion on the head, neck, hands, feet, pretibial, or anogenital areas, though it can also occur on the trunk and extremities [1, 5, 17, 28]. Lesions are often erythematous, scaly, nodular, ulcerated, or bleeding, and may arise from precursor lesions such as actinic keratoses or Bowen's disease [21, 29-31]. Symptoms can include pain, burning, stinging, anesthesia, paresthesia, facial paralysis, diplopia, or blurred vision, particularly when perineural invasion (PNI) is present [111, 112]. Rapid growth is a clinical feature that may indicate aggressive behavior [107, 108]. Signs on examination include well-defined or poorly-defined borders, induration, and surrounding erythema. High-risk features are suggested by larger tumor size (>2 cm), especially >4 cm, recurrent disease, immunosuppression, prior radiation or chronic inflammation, neurologic symptoms, poorly differentiated histology, high-risk subtypes (adenosquamous, sarcomatoid), deep invasion (>6 mm or beyond subcutaneous fat), PNI, and lymphovascular invasion [11, 34, 54, 74, 75, 85, 90, 92, 106, 114, 115, 131]. Atypical or missed presentations can occur in high-risk populations (e.g., organ transplant recipients, patients with lymphoma or HIV) where lesions may be difficult to assess clinically, leading to delayed diagnosis [45, 58-60, 100, 101]. Superficial biopsies may miss infiltrative histology present only at deeper margins [62, 63]. Lesions in the anogenital area may be mistaken for benign conditions, and actinic cheilitis on the lip requires careful evaluation [212-223]. Field cancerization, defined as UV-induced confluent dysplasia with diffuse actinic keratoses and superficial SCC, represents a precancerous state that warrants monitoring [140].
red_flags: Rapid growth tumor (growth rate >4 mm/month associated with higher risk of nodal progression) [107, 108].
Neurologic symptoms (pain, burning, anesthesia, paresthesia, facial paralysis, diplopia, blurred vision) suggesting perineural invasion [111, 112].
Immunosuppression (e.g., organ transplant recipients, lymphoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, HIV, drug-induced) [43-50].
High-risk anatomic location (head, neck, hands, feet, pretibial, anogenital area) [11, 34, 54, 55, 82, 86, 87].
Large tumor size (especially >2 cm, and particularly >4 cm) [34, 55, 85, 88, 90, 91].
Deep invasion (>6 mm thickness or invasion beyond subcutaneous fat) [11, 34, 54, 74, 75, 85, 89-91, 97, 129, 131].
Perineural invasion (especially tumor cells within nerve sheath of nerve deeper than dermis or caliber ≥0.1 mm) [11, 34, 54, 74, 75, 82, 88, 90, 106, 113-115, 132-138].
Site of prior radiation therapy or chronic inflammation [34, 35, 90, 92, 96, 103-106].
Palpable or radiographically suspicious regional lymph nodes [11, 82, 85, 97].

diagnostic.rationale: Initial assessment to evaluate lesion characteristics and identify risk factors.
diagnostic.key_findings: Documentation of lesion location, size, borders, growth rate, symptoms, immunosuppression status, history of prior skin cancers, and sun exposure.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for histologic confirmation and staging; superficial biopsies may miss invasive components.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic diagnosis of CSCC, degree of differentiation, depth of invasion, perineural invasion, lymphovascular invasion, and presence of high-risk subtypes.

diagnostic.rationale: May be utilized as needed to help identify lymphovascular or nerve invasion, or to identify single tumor cells or small aggregates.
diagnostic.key_findings: Enhanced detection of perineural or lymphovascular invasion when morphology is equivocal.

staging.test: Complete skin and regional lymph node examination
staging.rationale: To assess for additional primary tumors, in-transit lesions, or nodal metastases.
Preferred imaging modality for evaluating perineural disease or deep soft tissue involvement.
staging.indication: Suspicion of perineural spread, deep invasion, or assessment of extent of local disease.

staging.rationale: Preferred for evaluating bone disease; also used for nodal staging.
staging.indication: Suspicion of bone involvement, staging of regional lymph nodes, and for RT planning.

staging.rationale: Useful for evaluating nodal and distant metastatic disease.
staging.indication: Clinical suspicion of regional nodal or distant metastases.

staging.rationale: Can be used for assessment of regional lymph nodes; ultrasound-guided biopsy may be performed.
staging.indication: Evaluation of palpable or suspicious lymph nodes.

staging.test: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) or core biopsy of lymph nodes
staging.rationale: To obtain pathologic confirmation of nodal metastases.
staging.indication: Palpable or suspicious lymph nodes identified on exam or imaging.

staging.rationale: May be considered to confirm negative FNA or core biopsy if clinical suspicion remains high.
staging.indication: High clinical suspicion despite negative initial biopsy.

biomarkers.test: No specific serum biomarkers are established for CSCC.
biomarkers.rationale: Diagnosis and staging rely on histopathology and imaging.

do_not_miss: Adequate biopsy depth to include dermis for accurate staging.
Assessment and reporting of perineural invasion and lymphovascular invasion.
Complete skin and regional lymph node examination.
Appropriate imaging for high-risk features (e.g., MRI for perineural disease, CT for bone involvement).
avoid: Routine imaging in low-risk, localized CSCC without high-risk features.
Superficial biopsies that may underestimate invasion depth.
Unnecessary imaging without clinical suspicion of extensive disease.

staging.system: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition (2017) for cutaneous carcinoma of the head and neck [1, 2].

t_stages.description: Tumor >2 cm but ≤4 cm in greatest dimension.
Tumor >4 cm in maximum dimension or minor bone erosion or perineural invasion or deep invasion*.
Tumor with gross cortical bone/marrow invasion.
Tumor with skull base invasion and/or skull base foramen involvement.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Metastasis in a single ipsilateral lymph node, ≤3 cm in greatest dimension and ENE(−).
Metastasis in a single ipsilateral node >3 cm but ≤6 cm in greatest dimension and ENE(−).
Metastases in multiple ipsilateral nodes, none >6 cm in greatest dimension and ENE(−).
Metastases in bilateral or contralateral lymph nodes, none >6 cm in greatest dimension and ENE(−).
Metastasis in a lymph node >6 cm in greatest dimension and ENE(−).
Metastasis in any node(s) and clinically overt ENE [ENE(+)] or metastasis in any node(s) with ENE(+).

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Small tumor, low risk of metastasis.

stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with local therapy +/- adjuvant treatment based on risk factors.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced local disease or limited nodal involvement.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with multimodality therapy (surgery, RT, +/- systemic therapy).

stage_groups.criteria: T4, any N, M0; or any T, N2-3, M0; or any T, any N, M1.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very advanced local disease, extensive nodal involvement, or distant metastases.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative in metastatic setting; curative intent may be considered for selected cases with aggressive local-regional treatment.

staging_pearls: *Deep invasion is defined as invasion beyond the subcutaneous fat or >6 mm (as measured from the granular layer of adjacent normal epidermis to the base of the tumor) [131].

Perineural invasion for T3 classification is defined as tumor cells within the nerve sheath of a nerve lying deeper than the dermis or measuring ≥0.1 mm in caliber, or presenting with clinical or radiographic involvement of named nerves without skull base invasion or transgression [131].
A designation of 'U' or 'L' may be used for any N category to indicate metastasis above the lower border of the cricoid (U) or below (L) [131].
Clinical and pathological extranodal extension (ENE) should be recorded as ENE(−) or ENE(+) [131].

This staging system applies to cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, cutaneous carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma of the head and neck, and all other nonmelanoma skin carcinomas of the head and neck (except Merkel cell carcinoma) [1, 2].
Risk stratification for treatment decisions often incorporates additional factors beyond AJCC staging, such as immunosuppression, tumor location, and histologic features [SCC-2].

management_principles.overview: The primary goals of treatment for cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC) are complete tumor removal and maximal preservation of function and cosmesis. Treatment decisions should be customized to account for individual factors and patient preference. Surgical approaches often offer the most effective means for cure, but considerations of function, cosmesis, and patient preference may lead to choosing radiation therapy (RT), topical therapy, or systemic therapy as primary treatment. Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise is recommended during treatment planning for patients with high-risk, very-high-risk, and locally advanced CSCC (laCSCC). Epidermally limited CSCC may be treated with nonsurgical options [SCC-3, SCC-E].

by_intent.patient_group: All patients with local, regional, or locally advanced disease
by_intent.approach: Multimodal treatment tailored to risk stratification. Includes surgery (standard excision, Mohs micrographic surgery, or other forms of peripheral and deep en face margin assessment [PDEMA]), RT, and systemic therapy. For very-high-risk and laCSCC, neoadjuvant or adjuvant systemic therapy may be considered. For field cancerization, topical therapies are used.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with regional recurrence or distant metastatic disease
by_intent.approach: Multidisciplinary consultation to discuss systemic therapy alone or in combination with RT. Clinical trial participation is encouraged.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise is recommended for treatment planning of high-risk/very-high-risk CSCC, very-high-risk CSCC with significant risk of metastasis, laCSCC, and when managing positive margins. Multidisciplinary teams should discuss options including systemic therapy and RT in select cases for laCSCC and S-ITM.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: The guidelines do not specify explicit performance status criteria for treatment eligibility. However, the decision between curative surgery/RT and systemic therapy is influenced by patient fitness, comorbidities, extent of disease, and risk of functional or cosmetic defects. For nonsurgical candidates, definitive RT or systemic therapy should be considered.

management_pathways.title: Field Cancerization/Confluent Epidermal Dysplasia
branching_basis: Clinical presentation of diffuse actinic keratoses and superficial (in situ) SCC

nodes.criteria: Field cancerization defined as UV-induced confluent dysplasia clinically manifested as diffuse actinic keratoses and superficial (in situ) SCC

options.label: Sunscreen, Nicotinamide
Calcipotriene/Fluorouracil (preferred), Fluorouracil (preferred), Imiquimod, Tirbanibulin, Diclofenac (category 2B)

Ablative laser vermilionectomy or electrosurgical destruction, Ablative skin resurfacing (e.g., laser, dermabrasion), Chemical peels (Trichloroacetic acid), Cryotherapy, Curettage and electrodesiccation (C&E)
Capecitabine (for severe refractory disease), Photodynamic therapy (PDT) (e.g., topical Aminolevulinic acid [ALA], Porfimer sodium), Systemic retinoids (e.g., Acitretin, Isotretinoin)

management_pathways.title: Low-Risk Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma (CSCC)
branching_basis: Risk factors for local recurrence, metastases, or death (SCC-2)

nodes.criteria: Low-risk local CSCC: Trunk, extremities <2 cm; well-defined borders; primary tumor; no immunosuppression; no prior RT or chronic inflammation; no rapid growth; no neurologic symptoms; well or moderately differentiated; low-risk histologic subtype; depth <2 mm and no invasion beyond subcutaneous fat; no perineural involvement; no lymphatic or vascular involvement

options.label: Curettage and electrodesiccation (C&E), Shave removal, Mohs or other forms of PDEMA, Standard excision with 4- to 6-mm clinical margins and postoperative margin assessment
Consultation with radiation oncologist for nonsurgical candidates

management_pathways.title: High-Risk/Very-High-Risk CSCC Where Surgery or RT Has High Likelihood of Cure
branching_basis: Risk factors for local recurrence, metastases, or death (SCC-2)

nodes.criteria: High-risk/very-high-risk CSCC meeting criteria from SCC-2 with curative intent possible

options.label: Multidisciplinary consultation, Consider sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
Mohs or other forms of PDEMA (preferred for very high risk), Standard excision with wider surgical margins, Definitive RT for nonsurgical candidates
Re-resect if feasible, RT, If surgery and/or RT are not curative
Consider adjuvant RT if extensive perineural, large or named nerve involvement, or other poor prognostic features

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT can be considered for CSCCs with gross clinical radiologic PNI, multifocal histologic nerve invasion, ≥6 cm tumor diameter, recurrent tumors, high risk for regional or distant metastasis, close surgical margins where further surgery cannot be performed, and desmoplastic or infiltrative tumors in patients who are chronically immunosuppressed [e, oo].

management_pathways.title: Very-High-Risk CSCC with Significant Risk of Extensive Local Recurrence or Nodal Metastasis
branching_basis: Risk factors for local recurrence, metastases, or death (SCC-2)

nodes.criteria: Very-high-risk CSCC with significant risk of extensive local recurrence, nodal, or in-transit metastasis

options.label: Multidisciplinary consultation, Radiologic staging (MRI/CT/ultrasound), Consider SLNB, Consider neoadjuvant therapy with Cemiplimab-rwlc
Mohs or other forms of PDEMA (preferred), Standard excision with wider surgical margins, Definitive RT for nonsurgical candidates
Re-resect if feasible, RT, If surgery and/or RT are not curative
Consider adjuvant RT, Consider Cemiplimab-rwlc following adjuvant RT in patients with extremely high risk nodal and non-nodal features (category 1 preferred)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT as above. For patients with extremely high-risk features (extracapsular extension with largest node ≥20 mm or ≥3 involved nodes; or non-nodal features like in-transit metastases, T4 lesion with bone invasion, PNI, or locally recurrent tumor with ≥1 additional risk factor), Cemiplimab-rwlc following adjuvant RT is category 1 preferred [pp, c].

management_pathways.title: Locally Advanced Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma (laCSCC)
branching_basis: Definition: primary or recurrent extensive disease where surgery and/or RT may not result in cure or may produce significant functional impairment

nodes.criteria: laCSCC (primary or recurrent extensive disease where surgery and/or RT may not result in cure or would produce significant functional impairment)

options.label: Multidisciplinary discussion, Consider neoadjuvant therapy with Cemiplimab-rwlc and/or clinical trial
Surgery (Mohs/PDEMA or standard excision) after neoadjuvant therapy
RT ± systemic therapy, Systemic therapy if curative RT is not feasible
Re-resect if feasible, RT ± systemic therapy, Systemic therapy if curative RT is not feasible, Consider adjuvant RT for poor prognostic features

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT can be considered for high-risk features. For nonsurgical candidates, multidisciplinary teams discuss concurrent systemic therapy in select cases.

management_pathways.title: Clinical Staging, Preoperative Assessment, and Primary Treatment for Regional Lymph Nodes
branching_basis: Palpable or radiographically concerning lymph nodes

nodes.criteria: Palpable regional lymph nodes or abnormal lymph nodes identified by imaging studies

options.label: FNA or core biopsy, If negative: re-evaluate with CT and repeat biopsy
CT with contrast of nodal basin, Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT or FDG-PET/CT as clinically indicated
Regional lymph node dissection preferred unless patient is not surgical candidate
Imaging of anatomic region (CT with contrast), Multidisciplinary discussion
Consider neoadjuvant systemic therapy followed by resection, Upfront resection with consideration of adjuvant therapy
Systemic therapy ± RT, Isolated limb infusion/perfusion (ILI/ILP) with Melphalan-based regimens (Useful in Certain Circumstances)

H&P: For low risk: Every 3–12 mo for 2 y, then every 6–12 mo for 3 y, then annually for life. For high risk: Every 3–6 mo for 2 y, then every 6–12 mo for 3 y, then annually for life. For very high risk: Every 3–6 mo for 2 y, then every 6 mo for 3 y, then every 6–12 mo for life. Consider imaging if clinical exam insufficient or appreciable risk of subclinical recurrence. Patient education: Sun protection, Self-examination of skin.

H&P: Every 2–3 mo for 1 y, then every 2–4 mo for 1 y, then every 4–6 mo for 3 y, then every 6–12 mo for life. Consider imaging if clinical exam insufficient or appreciable risk of subclinical recurrence. Patient education: Sun protection, Self-examination of skin and lymph nodes.

Multidisciplinary consultation to discuss systemic therapy alone or in combination with RT. Consider palliative RT/surgery for symptomatic sites. Under highly selective circumstances, resection of limited metastases can be considered.

items.text: Complete history and physical examination (H&P), including assessment of primary tumor location, size, clinical borders, and presence of neurologic symptoms.
Examination of regional lymph nodes as indicated for suspicion of nodal disease.
Complete skin examination to identify concurrent precancers or cancers at other sun-exposed sites and to assess risk of cutaneous melanoma.
Biopsy that extends into the dermis for any suspicious lesion to confirm diagnosis and assess depth, differentiation, and high-risk histologic features (e.g., adenosquamous, sarcomatoid subtypes).

Pathologic evaluation ideally performed by a dermatologist, pathologist, or dermatopathologist experienced in cutaneous neoplasms. Reporting should include anatomic location, clinical diameter, primary vs. recurrent, clinical or radiologic nerve invasion, and risk factors (SCC-A).

Imaging studies of area of interest as indicated for suspicion of locally extensive or metastatic disease (e.g., deep structural involvement such as bone, perineural disease, deep soft tissue). MRI with and without contrast is preferred for perineural disease or deep soft tissue involvement. CT with contrast is preferred if bone disease is suspected.

For clinically or radiographically concerning regional lymph nodes: CT with contrast of the nodal basin to determine size, number, and location of nodes; chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast or FDG-PET/CT as clinically indicated to rule out distant disease.
MRI with and without contrast of the brain may be considered to rule out subclinical cortical involvement in cases with bone invasion.

Stratification to Determine Treatment Options for Local CSCC Based on Risk Factors for Local Recurrence, Metastases, or Death (SCC-2). Risk category assignment should be based on the highest risk factor present.

Assessment of risk factors including location/diameter, clinical borders, primary vs. recurrent, immunosuppression, site of prior RT or chronic inflammation, rapid growth tumor, neurologic symptoms, degree of differentiation, histologic subtype, depth, perineural involvement, and lymphatic or vascular involvement.
Multidisciplinary consultation at a center with specialized expertise for high-risk, very-high-risk, and locally advanced CSCC to discuss treatment options.

Consideration of sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) prior to or at time of PDEMA for patients with very-high-risk CSCCs that are recurrent or have multiple risk factors and have normal exam of draining nodal basin (category 2B).

surgery.role: Surgical approaches often offer the most effective and efficient means for accomplishing cure. The primary goals are complete tumor removal and maximal preservation of function and cosmesis.
principles: Surgical approaches are customized to account for individual factors, patient preference, tumor size, location, and risk features.
Tissue rearrangement (e.g., flap reconstruction, extensive undermining) should not be undertaken until clear margins are identified.
For tumors being considered for SLNB, delay reconstruction if not able to close primarily with minimal undermining.
If invasion to parotid fascia is noted, superficial parotidectomy may be indicated.

procedures.name: Curettage and Electrodesiccation (C&E)
procedures.approach: Fast and cost-effective technique for superficial lesions; does not allow histologic margin assessment.
procedures.indication: Low-risk CSCC. Should not be used to treat areas with terminal hair growth. If subcutaneous layer is reached, surgical excision should generally be performed instead.
procedures.key_complications: Lower cure rate than excision; risk of incomplete removal if tumor extends into subcutaneous tissue.

procedures.name: Mohs Micrographic Surgery or Excision with Peripheral and Deep En Face Margin Assessment (PDEMA)
procedures.approach: Surgical techniques allowing high-quality histologic visualization and interpretation of the entire marginal surface of excised tissue. Mohs is the most commonly used form of PDEMA. Must meet specific criteria (SCC-F).
procedures.indication: Preferred for very-high-risk CSCC. Also a primary treatment option for low-risk, high-risk/very-high-risk, and laCSCC following multidisciplinary discussion.
procedures.key_complications: Higher recurrence rates for recurrent tumors; risk of discrepancy between preoperative biopsy and Mohs findings.

procedures.name: Shave Removal
procedures.approach: Sharp removal by transverse bowl-shaped slicing to remove epidermal and dermal lesions without including fat; does not require suture closure.
procedures.indication: Low-risk CSCC on trunk or extremities when tumor does not extend beyond the dermis.
procedures.key_complications: Concern for inaccurate margin status assessment.

procedures.name: Standard Excision with Postoperative Margin Assessment
procedures.approach: Surgical excision with wider surgical margins (4-6 mm for low-risk lesions; wider margins for high-risk lesions) followed by postoperative pathologic evaluation of margins.
procedures.indication: Low-risk, high-risk/very-high-risk, very-high-risk, and laCSCC when Mohs/PDEMA is not available. Also for positive margins after other modalities if re-excision is feasible.
procedures.key_complications: Risk of positive margins; potential need for re-excision.

procedures.name: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB)
procedures.approach: Biopsy of the first draining lymph node basin to assess for subclinical nodal metastases.
procedures.indication: Consider in cases of recurrent or multiple high-risk features for very-high-risk CSCC with normal exam of draining nodal basin (category 2B).
procedures.key_complications: False-negative results; risk of postoperative recurrence/metastases despite completion lymphadenectomy.

procedures.name: Regional Lymph Node Dissection
procedures.approach: Surgical removal of regional lymph nodes.
procedures.indication: Preferred treatment for operable regional lymph node disease. Extent of surgery (e.g., parotidectomy, neck dissection) depends on number, location, and size of affected nodes.
procedures.key_complications: Morbidity related to extent of dissection.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Appropriate surgical margins should be determined case by case based on tumor and patient-specific factors. For low-risk lesions <2 cm, 4-mm clinical margins should result in complete removal in >95% of cases. For lesions >2 cm, 6-mm margins may be needed. For high-risk locations or features, margins of 4-9 mm may be required based on diameter.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: If clinical evaluation of incisional biopsy suggests that microstaging is inadequate, consider narrow margin excisional biopsy to obtain accurate measurement of thickness and anatomic level of invasion. Tissue rearrangement should not be undertaken until clear margins are identified.

radiation_therapy.role: RT is a primary treatment option for nonsurgical candidates or when patient preference and other factors lead to its choice. It is also used adjuvantly for positive margins or high-risk features after surgery, and for regional disease.
principles: Refer to the ASTRO Guideline on Definitive and Postoperative Radiation Therapy for Basal and Squamous Cell Cancers of the Skin for general indications and dose recommendations [1].
Protracted fractionation is associated with improved cosmetic results and should be utilized for poorly vascularized or cartilaginous areas.
For extensive perineural invasion (PNI) or involvement of named nerves (particularly in the head and neck region), consider including the course of the cranial nerve proximally.

Perineural tumor spread (PNTS), defined as clinically or radiographically apparent macroscopic spread along nerves, warrants comprehensive coverage of the involved and potentially interconnected cranial nerve pathways.

RT is contraindicated for genetic conditions predisposing to skin cancer (e.g., basal cell nevus syndrome [Gorlin syndrome]) and relatively contraindicated for patients with connective tissue diseases (e.g., scleroderma).
Given higher complication rates, reirradiation should not be routinely utilized for recurrent disease within a prior radiation field.
Consider elective nodal irradiation (ENI) in cases of multiple high-risk pathologic features, especially in immunocompromised patients.
Recommendations for external beam RT (EBRT) apply to both photons and electrons.
Image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT) is considered best practice when treating with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT), proton beam radiotherapy, or 3D conformal radiation.
Application of bolus over the primary tumor should be considered to ensure full dose to the surface.
Radiation treatments should be given by a practicing radiation oncologist with radiation physics support.

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant RT
dose_frameworks.schedule: Conventional fractionation (five daily treatments per week)
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative setting for positive margins or high-risk features.

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive EBRT Hypofractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: Definitive treatment, especially for older patients or those unable to attend protracted course.

dose_frameworks.name: Regional Disease Conventional
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative or definitive treatment of regional lymph node disease.

dose_frameworks.name: S-ITM RT
dose_frameworks.indication: For resected or unresected satellitosis/in-transit metastasis.

approaches.name: Definitive External Beam RT (EBRT)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: May be considered in select cases for laCSCC after multidisciplinary discussion.
approaches.indication: Nonsurgical candidates with local, regional, or locally advanced disease.
approaches.toxicities: Poor cosmetic outcomes (telangiectasia, skin pigmentation changes, fibrosis), non-healing ulcers (especially for SCC in situ), soft tissue/cartilage/bone necrosis, decreased sensation, cataracts (periorbital lesions).

approaches.name: Adjuvant EBRT
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Consider concurrent systemic therapy for high-risk regional disease with ENE or incompletely resected nodes.
approaches.indication: Positive margins after surgery or high-risk features (PNI, tumor ≥6 cm, recurrent, etc.).

approaches.name: Brachytherapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: Custom surface mold applicators: 3 Gy x 16 fractions (daily) or 5 Gy x 8 fractions (2x/week). Interstitial brachytherapy: Goal BED10 at least 60 Gy (e.g., 4.4 Gy x 10 fractions BID). Shielded single channel applicators: 3 Gy x 12–16 fractions (daily).
approaches.indication: Particularly for head and neck sites. Surface applicators for superficial tumors (<5 mm depth). Interstitial for deeper tumors.
approaches.toxicities: Potential for tissue necrosis, poor cosmesis.

approaches.name: Isolated Limb Infusion/Perfusion (ILI/ILP)
approaches.indication: Useful in certain circumstances for limb-only S-ITM with progression on/contraindications to standard therapies.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in various settings: neoadjuvant for very-high-risk or laCSCC, adjuvant for extremely high-risk features, concurrent with RT for select laCSCC or regional disease, and alone for unresectable, recurrent, or metastatic disease. Immune checkpoint inhibitors (cemiplimab-rwlc, pembrolizumab) are preferred in many settings.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Topical therapies for actinic keratoses and in situ SCC.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Topical Imiquimod, Topical Tirbanibulin, Topical Diclofenac (category 2B).

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For tumors with nonreactive non-keratoacanthomatous rapid growth, in-transit metastasis, borderline resectable, or where surgery alone may not be curative or may result in significant functional limitation.
For patients with extremely high-risk nodal and non-nodal features and postoperative negative margins.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: Extremely high-risk features defined as: nodal features (extracapsular extension with largest node ≥20 mm or ≥3 involved nodes) or non-nodal features (in-transit metastases, T4 lesion with bone invasion, PNI, or locally recurrent tumor with ≥1 additional risk factor) [pp, c].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: If curative RT or surgery is not feasible for locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cosibelimab-ipdl (if curative RT or surgery is not feasible for locally advanced [category 2B] or metastatic disease), Nivolumab. If ineligible for or progressed on immune checkpoint inhibitors: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel, Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Cetuximab, EGFR inhibitors (e.g., Cetuximab). Useful in certain circumstances: Neoadjuvant Cemiplimab-rwlc, Capecitabine, Cisplatin, Cisplatin/Fluorouracil.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Systemic therapy alone (Table 3) or in combination with local therapies such as RT or surgery (Table 2)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For unresectable or incompletely resected S-ITM.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Isolated limb infusion/perfusion (ILI/ILP) with Melphalan-based regimens (Useful in Certain Circumstances).

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: RT ± systemic therapy (Table 2) for resected high-risk regional disease of head and neck. Systemic therapy alone (Table 3) if curative RT is not feasible.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For unresectable, inoperable, or incompletely resected regional disease.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Regional Recurrence or Distant Metastatic Disease
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Systemic therapy alone (Table 3) or in combination with RT (Table 2)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For regional recurrence or distant metastases.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Clinical trial. For symptomatic sites: palliative RT/surgery. Under highly selective circumstances: resection of limited metastases.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin
key_regimens.setting: Systemic therapy for use with RT or surgery (Table 2).

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin
key_regimens.setting: Systemic therapy for use with RT or surgery (Table 2).

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel
key_regimens.setting: Systemic therapy for use with RT or surgery (Table 2) or alone (Table 3).

key_regimens.name: Cetuximab
key_regimens.setting: EGFR inhibitor for use with RT or alone (Table 2 and 3).
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Infusion reactions, acneiform rash, hypomagnesemia, diarrhea.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Fluorouracil
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nephrotoxicity, myelosuppression, mucositis, diarrhea, hand-foot syndrome.

key_regimens.name: Cemiplimab-rwlc
key_regimens.setting: Preferred for locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC; neoadjuvant; adjuvant after RT for extremely high-risk features.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) including pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, skin reactions.

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: Preferred for locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC if curative RT or surgery is not feasible.

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab
key_regimens.setting: Other recommended for locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC if curative RT or surgery is not feasible.

key_regimens.name: Capecitabine
key_regimens.setting: Useful in certain circumstances for severe refractory field cancerization or metastatic disease.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Follow-up and Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Follow-up schedules are based on risk status and occur at regular intervals after primary treatment. 70% to 80% of CSCC recurrences develop within 2 years of initial therapy, so close follow-up during this period is critical.
response_logic: Local disease: H&P with complete skin and regional lymph node exam at intervals based on risk (low, high, very high). Consider imaging if clinical exam insufficient or appreciable risk of subclinical recurrence.
Regional/S-ITM disease: H&P with complete skin and regional lymph node exam at intervals. Consider imaging as above.

Recurrence: Local recurrence treated per pathways for high-risk/very-high-risk CSCC (SCC-5 and SCC-6). Locally advanced recurrence per SCC-7. New regional disease per SCC-8 and SCC-10. Regional recurrence or distant metastases per multidisciplinary consultation.
imaging_recommendations: Imaging modality and targeted area at discretion of treating team based on suspected extent of disease (local, regional, metastatic).
Histologic confirmation sufficient for local recurrence, but MRI with and without contrast can assess extent of local disease.
For nodal or distant metastases, histologic analysis and/or other imaging modalities for confirmation and gauge extent of disease.
Surveillance imaging of regional nodal basin and to evaluate for distant metastatic disease, ideally based on multidisciplinary board recommendation.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Actinic keratoses that have atypical appearance or do not respond to appropriate therapy should be biopsied for histologic evaluation.
For positive margins after surgery, re-excision if feasible, or RT, or systemic therapy if curative RT not feasible.
Under highly selective circumstances and in context of multidisciplinary consultation, resection of limited metastases can be considered.

prognosis.overall: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (CSCC) generally has a good prognosis, with a 5-year survival rate of approximately 98%. However, treatment continues to evolve to improve quality of life and minimize recurrence. Metastatic CSCC is rare, occurring in an estimated 1.9% to 2.7% of all cases, with nodal metastases and distant metastatic disease estimated at 3.7% and 0.4%, respectively. One-third of deaths from CSCC occur from local disease alone.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 3-year disease-specific survival (DSS) rates are 93% for lesions <4 cm and 67% for lesions ≥4 cm (P = .0003).
by_stage.context: Based on a large prospective study.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: Prospective data show metastasis rates of 0% for tumors ≤2.0 mm in thickness, 4% for tumors 2.1 to 6.0 mm, and 16% for tumors >6.0 mm.
by_stage.context: Risk of recurrence and metastasis is significantly higher for lesions with thickness >2 mm.

by_stage.stage: Depth of invasion beyond subcutaneous fat
by_stage.five_yr_survival: Invasion into the subcutaneous fat significantly increases rates of recurrence and metastasis.

Perineural invasion (PNI) is associated with increased risks of recurrence, metastasis, and death. Poorer outcomes are associated with the presence of clinical symptoms and extent of neuronal involvement.
by_stage.context: Tumor cells within the nerve sheath of a nerve lying deeper than the dermis or measuring ≥0.1 mm.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-year disease-free survival (DFS) and disease-specific survival (DSS) estimates range from 59% to 83% and 63% to 83%, respectively, for patients with regional involvement treated with surgery plus adjuvant radiation therapy.
by_stage.context: Regional nodal involvement significantly increases the risk of recurrence and mortality.

by_stage.five_yr_survival: 5-year survival is 52% for immunosuppressed patients (e.g., transplant recipients or those with hematologic malignancies), compared to 72% for immunocompetent patients.

prognostic_factors: Anatomic location (head, neck, hands, feet, pretibia, anogenital area are high-risk)
Tumor diameter (≥2 cm and especially ≥4 cm)
Site of prior radiation therapy or chronic inflammation
Depth of invasion (>6 mm or invasion beyond subcutaneous fat)
Perineural involvement (tumor cells within nerve sheath of a nerve deep to dermis or ≥0.1 mm caliber)
prognosis.prognostic_context: Risk category assignment (low, high, very high) for local CSCC is based on the highest risk factor present. The high-risk group has elevated risk of local recurrence; the very-high-risk group has elevated risk of local recurrence and metastasis. Other staging systems (AJCC 8th edition, Brigham and Women's Hospital) can also define high-risk groups. Patients at high risk for multiple primary CSCCs (e.g., organ transplant recipients, immunosuppressed, genetic syndromes) may develop more aggressive tumors.

disease_related.management: Managed per the appropriate pathway for primary treatment (SCC-5, SCC-6).
Multidisciplinary discussion and multimodality treatment including neoadjuvant and adjuvant therapy (SCC-7).
Multidisciplinary discussion; options include systemic therapy with or without local therapies such as radiation therapy or surgery (SCC-9).
Surgical excision of primary tumor and regional lymph node dissection, with consideration of adjuvant radiation therapy and systemic therapy depending on pathologic findings (SCC-8, SCC-10).
Multidisciplinary consultation to discuss systemic therapy alone or in combination with radiation therapy; consider clinical trials (SCC-11).
Surgical planning to maximize preservation of function and cosmesis; reconstructive surgery may be required.

immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) such as colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic toxicity (pembrolizumab, cemiplimab-rwlc, nivolumab, cosibelimab-ipdl)
Risk of organ rejection in transplant recipients
targeted_therapy: Acneiform rash, paronychia, infusion reactions (EGFR inhibitors: cetuximab, panitumumab)
surgery: Functional impairment (e.g., facial nerve damage during parotidectomy)
radiation: Late: Telangiectasia, skin pigmentation changes, fibrosis, non-healing ulcers, soft tissue/cartilage/bone necrosis, decreased sensation, cataracts (for periorbital lesions)

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care for CSCC patients focuses on managing treatment-related toxicities, preserving function, and ensuring quality of life. Multidisciplinary involvement is key.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Not explicitly detailed in the source; however, nutritional support may be necessary for patients undergoing intensive chemotherapy or experiencing significant mucositis or dysphagia.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not specified in the source. General antiemetic guidelines for chemotherapy regimens (e.g., 5-HT3 antagonists, dexamethasone) should be followed.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not specified in the source. Use of granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) should follow institutional guidelines for chemotherapy-induced neutropenia prophylaxis.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not explicitly mentioned. Venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis should be considered per standard risk assessment and institutional protocols, especially during periods of reduced mobility or with certain chemotherapies.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain management is not detailed but is an essential component. Analgesics should be tailored to the type and severity of pain, considering potential neuropathic components from perineural invasion or treatment.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Patient education is emphasized, including sun protection, self-examination, and understanding of the disease. Psychological support may be needed to address body image concerns, anxiety, or depression related to diagnosis and treatment.
supportive_care.dental_care: Not mentioned. Dental care may be relevant for patients with head and neck CSCC, especially those receiving radiation therapy to the head and neck region.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: Follow-up schedules are stratified by risk category: for low-risk patients, every 3–12 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years, then annually for life; for high-risk patients, every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years, then annually for life; for very-high-risk patients, every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 3 years, then every 6–12 months for life. This is based on the high rate of recurrence within the first 2 years (70%–80% of recurrences develop within 2 years).
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Continued long-term surveillance is essential because 13%–50% of patients will develop another SCC within 5 years, representing at least a 10-fold increase in risk compared to the general population. Patients with prior SCC are also at increased risk of developing cutaneous melanoma and BCC. Close follow-up during the first 2 years is critical due to the peak recurrence period.
late_effects_screening: Screening for secondary malignancies, especially in patients who received prior radiation therapy.
Monitoring for long-term radiation sequelae: telangiectasia, fibrosis, non-healing ulcers, necrosis, decreased sensation, cataracts.
Assessment of functional and cosmetic outcomes after surgery or radiation.
Surveillance for treatment-related toxicities from systemic therapies (e.g., endocrinopathies from immunotherapy).
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most recurrences occur within 2 years of initial therapy. Local recurrence rates vary by treatment modality (e.g., 3.1% for primary CSCCs treated with Mohs). Recurrent tumors are at higher risk of metastasis. Risk factors for recurrence after treatment include larger subclinical extension, more Mohs stages, perineural invasion, and certain histologic features.

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Low-risk: Every 3–12 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years, then annually for life.
High-risk: Every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3 years, then annually for life.
Very-high-risk: Every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months for 3 years, then every 6–12 months for life.
Patients with regional/S-ITM disease: Every 2–3 months for 1 year, then every 2–4 months for 1 year, then every 4–6 months for 3 years, then every 6–12 months for life.
Complete skin and regional lymph node examination at each visit.
imaging_strategy: Imaging is not routine for all patients. Consider imaging if clinical exam is insufficient for following disease or if there is appreciable risk of subclinical local or nodal recurrence.
For locally extensive or metastatic disease: MRI with and without contrast is preferred for perineural disease or deep soft tissue involvement; CT with contrast is preferred for bone disease.
For nodal or distant metastases: histologic analysis and/or other imaging modalities (CT, FDG-PET/CT) can be used for confirmation and to gauge extent of disease.
Imaging modality and targeted area should be at the discretion of the treating team based on the suspected extent of disease.
surveillance.laboratory_monitoring: Not specified in the source. Routine laboratory monitoring is not detailed; however, standard laboratory tests may be performed as clinically indicated during systemic therapy.
supportive_follow_up: Patient education: strict sun protection, self-examination of the skin (monthly), and for those with regional disease, self-examination of lymph nodes.

Follow-up with a dermatologist is strongly recommended if the patient meets criteria: past or imminent solid organ, marrow, or hematopoietic cell transplant; ≥1 cutaneous melanomas in the past 5 years; or ≥4 nonmelanoma skin cancers in the past 5 years.
Adjust frequency of follow-up based on risk of recurrence or development of new primary tumors.
Consider prophylactic measures in high-risk patients (e.g., oral retinoids, nicotinamide).

special_situations.situation: Organ transplant recipients and other immunosuppressed patients
special_situations.key_message: These patients are at high risk for developing multiple CSCCs and aggressive tumors. Risk is linked to duration and dose of immunosuppression.
special_situations.management: Consider decreasing immunosuppression and/or switching to mTOR inhibitors (e.g., sirolimus, everolimus) in consultation with transplant physician. More frequent follow-up may be needed. Destructive therapies may be preferred for multiple low-risk tumors. Adjuvant radiation therapy is used more frequently.
special_situations.evidence: Prospective randomized trials show switching to mTOR inhibitors reduces new CSCC risk. Analyses of transplant registries report 5-fold to 113-fold increased SCC incidence.

special_situations.key_message: UV-induced confluent dysplasia manifests as diffuse actinic keratoses and superficial SCC. Patients are at high risk for multiple primary CSCCs.
special_situations.management: Treat actinic keratoses at first development. Preferred topical therapies: calcipotriene/fluorouracil or fluorouracil alone. Other options: imiquimod, tirbanibulin, diclofenac (category 2B). Destructive therapies: ablative laser, chemical peels, cryotherapy. Systemic options: capecitabine for severe refractory disease, photodynamic therapy, oral retinoids (acitretin, isotretinoin). Prevention: daily sunscreen, oral nicotinamide.
special_situations.evidence: Topical fluorouracil plus calcipotriol shows longest duration of prophylaxis against SCC. Oral nicotinamide reduces new AKs and SCCs.

special_situations.situation: Genetic syndromes predisposing to CSCC (e.g., xeroderma pigmentosum, recessive dystrophic epidermolysis bullosa)
special_situations.key_message: Rare genetic syndromes markedly predispose to aggressive CSCC. Patients should be referred to a cancer center with expertise in CSCC prevention and prophylaxis.
special_situations.management: Specialized multidisciplinary care. Intensive sun protection and frequent surveillance. Chemoprophylaxis with oral retinoids may be considered.
special_situations.evidence: Patients with xeroderma pigmentosum have a >1000-fold increased risk of skin cancer.

special_situations.key_message: PNI is associated with increased risks of recurrence, metastasis, and death. Large nerve involvement (≥0.1 mm or deeper than dermis) is a very-high-risk feature.
special_situations.management: For extensive PNI or involvement of named nerves (especially in head and neck), consider including the course of the cranial nerve proximally in radiation therapy. Consider adjuvant radiation therapy for high-risk features. Consider referral to high-volume center for radiation given potential toxicity.
special_situations.evidence: PNI is a prognostic factor for poor outcomes. Recurrence rates after Mohs surgery are elevated (6.8%–32.3%).

special_situations.key_message: Recurrent disease is high-risk regardless of other features. Higher rates of local recurrence and metastasis compared to primary tumors.
special_situations.management: Multidisciplinary consultation. Treatment based on risk stratification (SCC-5, SCC-6). Consider adjuvant radiation therapy if high-risk features present.
special_situations.evidence: Recurrent tumors have higher rates of local recurrence and metastasis after treatment.

clinical_pearls: Risk category assignment for local CSCC should be based on the highest risk factor present. The high-risk group has elevated risk of local recurrence; the very-high-risk group has elevated risk of both local recurrence and metastasis.

Mohs micrographic surgery (or other forms of PDEMA) is the preferred surgical technique for very-high-risk CSCC because it allows intraoperative analysis of 100% of the excision margin, leading to lower recurrence rates.

For patients with very-high-risk nodal and non-nodal features and postoperative negative margins following adjuvant radiation therapy, adjuvant cemiplimab-rwlc is category 1 preferred (based on a phase III trial showing improved DFS).

Neoadjuvant cemiplimab-rwlc should be considered for very-high-risk tumors with nonreactive non-keratoacanthomatous rapid growth, in-transit metastasis, borderline resectability, or where surgery alone may not be curative or may result in significant functional limitation.
Immunosuppressed patients (especially transplant recipients) are at significantly higher risk for multiple and aggressive CSCCs; consider modifying immunosuppression and using mTOR inhibitors.
Regular sunscreen use and oral nicotinamide may reduce the development of new AKs and CSCCs in high-risk patients.
Follow-up schedules should be tailored to risk category and adjusted based on the frequency of tumor development. Most recurrences occur within 2 years of initial therapy.

For radiation therapy, protracted fractionation is associated with improved cosmetic results and should be utilized for poorly vascularized or cartilaginous areas. Re-irradiation should not be routinely utilized for recurrent disease within a prior radiation field.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Squamous Cell Skin Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 2.2026. Includes detailed algorithms, principles of treatment, pathology, radiation therapy, and systemic therapy.

guidelines_resources.title: ASTRO Guideline on Definitive and Postoperative Radiation Therapy for Basal and Squamous Cell Cancers of the Skin
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for general indications and dose recommendations for radiation therapy.

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides TNM staging classification for cutaneous carcinoma of the head and neck, including CSCC.

guidelines_resources.organization: European Dermatology Forum (EDF), European Organisation for Research and Treatment of Cancer (EORTC), European Federation of the Nations of Cancer Institutes (EANCI), European Society of Surgical Oncology (ESSO)
guidelines_resources.title: European Interdisciplinary Guideline on Invasive Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Skin
guidelines_resources.notes: Cited for alternative management approaches and margin recommendations.

guidelines_resources.title: Guidelines for the Management of People with Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma
guidelines_resources.notes: Cited for margin recommendations and management strategies.

guidelines_resources.title: Find a Genetic Counselor Tool
guidelines_resources.notes: For locating genetic counseling services for patients with rare genetic syndromes predisposing to CSCC.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Cemiplimab or Placebo in High-Risk Cutaneous Squamous-Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Patients with local or regional CSCC after postoperative radiation therapy at high risk for recurrence due to nodal or non-nodal features.
key_trials.intervention: Adjuvant cemiplimab (350 mg every 3 weeks for 12 weeks, then 700 mg every 6 weeks for up to 36 weeks) or placebo.
key_trials.key_result: At median follow-up of 24 months, estimated DFS was 87% with cemiplimab vs 64% with placebo.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Cemiplimab led to lower risks of locoregional recurrence and distant recurrence; higher grade ≥3 adverse events than placebo (23.9% vs 14.2%).
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant cemiplimab as category 1 preferred for patients with extremely high-risk nodal and non-nodal features following adjuvant radiation therapy.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab for Locally Advanced and Recurrent/Metastatic Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Patients with locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC.
key_trials.key_result: ORR of 34% to 50%, complete response (CR) of 4% to 17%, partial response (PR) of 25% to 33%.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Safety and efficacy in different subgroups.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported pembrolizumab as a preferred systemic therapy option for locally advanced, recurrent, or metastatic CSCC when curative surgery or radiation is not feasible.

key_trials.full_name: Cemiplimab in Locally Advanced Cutaneous Squamous Cell Carcinoma: Phase 2, Single-Arm Trial
key_trials.key_result: ORR of 44% to 54%, CR of 0% to 13%, PR of 31% to 50%.
key_trials.practice_change: Established cemiplimab as a preferred systemic therapy option for locally advanced CSCC when surgery or radiation is not feasible.

key_trials.full_name: Neoadjuvant Cemiplimab for Stage II to IV Cutaneous Squamous-Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Patients with stage II to IV CSCC considered for surgery.
key_trials.intervention: Neoadjuvant cemiplimab (3 mg/kg every 2 weeks for up to 4 doses) prior to surgery.
key_trials.practice_change: Supported consideration of neoadjuvant cemiplimab for borderline resectable, unresectable, or high-morbidity surgery cases.

key_trials.full_name: First-Line Nivolumab in Patients with Locally Advanced or Metastatic Cutaneous Squamous-Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.intervention: Nivolumab 240 mg every 2 weeks or 480 mg every 4 weeks.
key_trials.key_result: Best ORR of 58.3%, no CRs reported. Median PFS 12.7 months, median OS 20.7 months.
key_trials.practice_change: Provided evidence for nivolumab as an other recommended systemic therapy option.

key_trials.full_name: Randomized Trial of Calcipotriol Combined with 5-Fluorouracil for Skin Cancer Precursor Immunotherapy
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Cumulative probability of remaining free from disease progression over 1500 days.
key_trials.key_result: Calcipotriol/5-FU group had significantly higher proportion disease-free at 1500 days. Fewer participants developed CSCC on treated face/scalp within 3 years (7% vs 28%; HR 0.215; P = .032).
key_trials.practice_change: Established topical calcipotriene/fluorouracil as a preferred treatment for field cancerization.
